title: Distributed ledgers and the governance of money
abstract: Distributed ledgers promise to enable the classical vision of money as a universal transaction record. But is it ever optimal to update a ledger through decentralized consensus? Analyzing an exchange economy with credit, we show that centralized updating is optimal when long-term rewards are more valued, minimizing redundant validation costs and maximizing economic surplus. Decentralization becomes preferable under weaker intertemporal incentives and when validators are drawn from market participants. We show how competing ledgers – anonymous or identified, permissioned or permissionless – can achieve socially optimal outcomes even in low-trust environments. Our framework provides a foundation for designing robust and efficient ledger systems.

author.Cyril Monnet*: University of Bern and Study Center Gerzensee, Switzerland

article.1. Introduction: Money, whether in the form of clay pots, metal coins, or banknotes, is a social convention that serves as a record of goods sold or services rendered in the past. In seminal contributions to monetary theory, Kocherlakota (1998) and Kocherlakota and Wallace (1998) show that the social convention of money can replicate many of the allocations that are feasible in a universal ledger of all previous transactions in the economy.In practice, trusted intermediaries, such as central banks and commercial banks, sustain money by maintaining and transferring balances among depositors. This centralized record-keeping system has been the mainstay of today’s monetary system. Recently, the advent of blockchain technology suggests that the authority to update the ledger can also be delegated to a decentralized network of validators that can function “without the need for a trusted third party” (e.g. see Nakamoto, 2008, Buterin, 2014a or Auer et al., 2024). This raises the broader research question of whether and under what circumstances it is economically efficient to implement market designs in which the updating of the ledger is to some extent decentralized (i.e., there is more than one authority in charge of updating the ledger).We address these questions in a credit economy in which some agents produce early for some other agents and expect these beneficiaries to reciprocate at a later date. However, when the latter “late” producers cannot commit to reciprocating, trade cannot be sustained in equilibrium. In similar contexts, Kocherlakota (1998) shows that trustless exchange can be sustained as an equilibrium when agents can freely consult a record-keeping device tracking the default history of the beneficiaries, as long as the device automatically updates itself according to the behavior of the beneficiaries (see also Rocheteau and Nosal, 2017). However, Kocherlakota (1998) sidesteps the questions of who should be responsible for ensuring the legitimacy of the ledger and how to guarantee that the authorities in charge have the proper incentives to ensure its integrity and consistency.Our contribution is to address these questions by focusing on the economic principles underlying the validation protocol. In doing so, we revisit Juvenal’s analysis of who – or what mechanism – should guard the guardians of the ledger in such a setting1: the agents in charge of managing the ledger – the “validators” – should accurately verify transactions and correctly update the ledger. The ledger could be centrally managed by a single authority, by committees formed of a limited number of privileged agents,2 or could be managed by every participant of a decentralized economy.3 There are two basic frictions in our setup that determine the optimal number of validators. First, there is no technical way to force a validator to sign any given transaction. Validation may be costly, especially if this involves costly monitoring of off-chain events.4 Validators, therefore, need incentives to actively verify and take part in the validation of transactions. Second, nothing can technically prevent a validator from endorsing two or more ledgers with conflicting histories.5 In this context, we analyze how incentives for validators can address the two frictions that technology alone cannot solve.As in Abadi and Brunnermeier (2018), Amoussou-Guénou et al. (2019), and Halaburda et al. (2021), ensuring the integrity and consistency of the ledger is a quintessential design issue because our economy cannot generate value without it. We examine theoretically how the optimal validation protocol deals with the frictions embedded in the consensus mechanism underlying the monetary system and derive the optimal number of validators, their compensation, and the optimal voting rule among validators. In turn, we can determine how the optimal validation protocol impacts the level of trade and hence the realized surplus in the economy. We endogenize the validation of records on the ledger and how consensus on actual transactions is reached as an equilibrium outcome in a repeated game setting. We assume validators are in charge of reading and updating the ledger of trade histories, and the consensus mechanism relies on supermajority voting.6 Since verification and communication are costly activities, validators must be compensated for their efforts. And since validators cannot be trusted to work, or to refuse bribes to falsify an entry, reaching consensus as an equilibrium actually requires that validators be compensated in excess of costs; i.e. they are paid rents.We analyze the optimal design of the trade and validation mechanisms as the solution to a fictitious planner’s problem, where optimality is defined in terms of maximizing the surplus from the trade net of the validation costs. The optimal mechanism chooses the number of validators, the supermajority threshold, the compensation of validators, and the trade allocation that maximize the gains from trade, subject to incentive compatibility conditions. We consider internal validation, where validators are also users of the system, as well as external validation, where they are not. We analyze variants of the technology, where users are identified or remain anonymous. We also examine how competing ledgers can implement the optimal design.We find that decentralizing record-keeping (i.e. having more than a single validator) can be more efficient than relying on a single intermediary. However, such improved governance does not come for free; ensuring incentives for the validators is costly and requires giving up unanimity and therefore possibly uses only a weaker form of consensus.Intertemporal incentives are key in determining the optimal solution. Validators’ incentives are enhanced by the reward they receive for validating transactions, and that reward should be high enough to deter them from ever falsifying the ledger. When intertemporal incentives are strong in the sense that the present value of future rewards is high, validators would have much to lose from misbehaving. In this case, a single validator who earns a large rent can be entrusted with managing the ledger.7 But precisely when intertemporal incentives are weak, it becomes prohibitively costly to prevent a single validator from misbehaving. The optimal design increases the number of validators and reduces the size of each transaction to lower the incentives for bribing.The many validators play a consensus game that has attributes of a public good provision game – consensus is deemed to be reached if and only if a supermajority of validators agree – which we proceed to solve using global game methods (see Carlsson and van Damme (1993) and Morris and Shin, 1998, Morris and Shin, 2003). Validators reach a given level of consensus as a unique, dominance solvable equilibrium if and only if the reward they earn is above a certain threshold.8We also show that decentralization naturally leads to a “stakeholder economy” in which the participants of the system manage the record-keeping themselves: only internal validation can support trade as an equilibrium with more than one validator. There are hence economies of scope in trading and validation. Achieving good governance and honest record-keeping is made easier by having validators who also participate in the market themselves and thus have an intrinsic interest in ensuring it goes smoothly.We consider both permissionless and permissioned variants of the technology, as well as identified and anonymous versions, focusing on the economic principles underlying the validation protocol. We also model the coexistence of multiple ledgers. In the model presented in the main section, ledgers are set up with a fixed one-time cost. In Online Appendix 1, we consider the case where the identity of validators is known and there is no cost to be a validator.9 In both cases, the mechanism decides whether validation should be permissioned or permissionless. Also on the side of the users, there can be either complete identification or complete (pseudo)anonymity. In all cases, users can switch between multiple ledgers.We show that competing ledgers can implement the social planner’s (constrained) optimal design. This is natural because competing ledgers will seek to maximize the expected utility of their users, and they charge a non-distortive lump-sum fee to cover the setup cost.10We examine potentially anonymous settings where users of the ledger (be it validators or normal users) open accounts at a cost. Only the account and its activities are recorded on the ledger, but the underlying identity of the account holder is not known. This implies that while an account can be closed (as punishment when the owner defaults), it is not possible to prevent users from opening another account to conduct trades in the future. We show that also in this setup, anonymous validators verify transactions and maintain ledger integrity to safeguard the value of their good standing on the ledger. For validators who earn rents, the cost of becoming a validator (by opening a validator’s account), which is part of the mechanism, needs to be high enough to remove their incentive to accepting a bribe. The underlying mechanism shows some similarity of this case in the model to the literature examining the set of consensus mechanisms based on so-called “proof of stake”, the process of achieving agreement on the state of the ledger by pledging collateral on an outcome (see, e.g., Abadi and Brunnermeier, 2021 and Saleh, 2020). The cost to open a validator’s account is similar to the opportunity cost of staked coins.11In addition to studying the general question of the optimality of decentralized record-keeping, our approach also helps us understand the so-called “oracle problem”, which relates to the challenge of incorporating off-chain information onto a blockchain. Blockchains rely on oracles because they are inherently blind to the outside world, as emphasized in Caldarelli (2020). On platforms such as Ethereum, oracles serve as reference points for external information – such as asset prices, interest rate benchmarks, or other relevant variables such as the official inflation rate (see Xu et al. (2016) for an introduction to oracles). Such oracles – centralized and dependable third parties – act as a link between blockchain technology and the outside world.12 In one interpretation of our model, trade and production can be viewed as off-chain events, and validators function as the oracles who confirm the validity of these events. However, since validators provide information used as an input to calculate the payouts of self-executing (i.e. smart) financial contracts, as oracles, they are easy targets for manipulation.13 These oracles must be compensated for integrating off-chain data, particularly because verifying this information is costly. To ensure the accuracy of the data they provide, it is crucial that oracles earn sufficient rent.Relation to the literature.A sizeable literature analyzes the incentives of miners in Bitcoin and similar cryptocurrencies that are based on a “proof-of-work” protocol.14 Kroll et al. (2013) and Prat and Walter (2018) examine free entry and the dynamics of the “mining” market,15 while Easley et al. (2019) and Huberman et al. (2021) examine the economics of the transaction market. Budish (forthcoming) and Chiu and Koeppl (2022) show that ensuring the finality of transactions in Bitcoin is very costly, as so-called “majority” or “history reversion” attacks are inherently profitable, while Auer (2019) examines whether the transaction market can generate sufficient miner income to ensure finality.16 Further to this, even in the absence of incentives to reverse history, sunspot equilibria can arise in proof-of work-based blockchains (Biais et al., 2019).17The literature on validator incentives and the design of permissioned versions of distributed ledgers is more sparse.18 Townsend (2020) focuses on an economics-based approach to the issue of distributed ledgers, exploring novel contracting possibilities enabled by DLT. Most closely related to our analysis are Abadi and Brunnermeier (2018), Halaburda et al. (2021), and Amoussou-Guénou et al. (2019). The latter authors first modeled the interaction between validators as a game entailing non-observable effort to check transactions and costly voting. They also analyzed this game in terms of moral hazard and public good provision. Closely related, Abadi and Brunnermeier (2018) and Halaburda et al. (2021) examine the incentives to reach consensus using communication games and their robustness if rational nodes can freely send messages to selected recipients only.Relative to their analysis, our focus is not on the specific steps of the communication game needed to reach consensus. Rather, our contribution is to link the ledger validation game to monetary exchange in a repeated game setting. We establish the uniqueness of the equilibrium via a global game approach, and characterize the optimal mechanism design, in particular in terms of the number of validators, size of transactions, and optimal supermajority voting threshold. In our work, all validators are profit-seeking, and the issue at heart is how the market can be designed so that profit-seeking validators actually verify the ledger and validate only correct histories.19 The focus on dealing with free-riding and coordination relates to several classical strands of papers on the coordination with many actors. Reminiscent of Grossman and Stiglitz (1976), free-riding can prevail in the case of multiple validators. Consistent with Biais et al. (2019) and Amoussou-Guénou et al. (2019), we also derive a folk theorem in Online Appendix 3: in this extension, validators have an incentive to pretend to verify without exerting the actual monitoring effort, which jeopardizes the legitimacy of the whole ledger. As validators become more patient, the free-rider problem has no bite and any allocation satisfying the validators’ participation constraint can be implemented in our strategic setup.Our paper also has ramifications in the banking literature, starting with Diamond (1984) or Williamson, 1986, Williamson, 1987 where banks are modeled as a way to save on monitoring costs.20 Another approach, pioneered by Leland and Pyle (1977) and developed by Boyd and Prescott (1986) models banks as information-sharing coalitions. Gu et al. (2013) show that higher rents can discipline intermediaries, while Huang (2019) uses that model to study the optimal number of intermediaries when they have an incentive to divert deposits. A related analysis that focuses on the optimal composition of the money stock between inside and outside money can be found in Monnet (2006), Cavalcanti and Wallace, 1999a, Cavalcanti and Wallace, 1999b, and Wallace (2005). Global games techniques have also been introduced in the banking literature to study the probability of a bank run occurring, e.g. by Rochet and Vives (2004) and Goldstein and Pauzner (2005).The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 lays down the basic setup and characterizes benchmark allocations absent a record-keeping device and a freely accessible one. Section 3 defines incentive-feasible allocation with DLT and characterizes the optimal allocation including the optimal number of validators.
article.2. The model: Our model builds on Gu et al. (2013).21 Time is discrete and infinite. The discount factor is β̃∈(0,1). Each period is divided into two distinct production/consumption stages, early and late, with one good per stage, the “early good” and the “late good”. Goods are non-storable across stages or across periods. There is a continuum of agents with two permanent types. There is a unit mass of early producers and another unit mass of late producers.22 Early producers can produce the early good that late producers like to consume. Late producers can produce the late good that early producers like to consume. Early and late producers have a survival probability σ at the end of a period. They learn whether they survive at the very end of the period so that their effective discount factor is β=σβ̃. Exiting agents are replaced by new agents of the same type so that the distribution of types is stationary.Preferences of early and late producers are represented by the following utility functions, respectively:23 Ue(xe,ye)=xe−ye,Uℓ(xℓ,yℓ)=u(xℓ)−yℓ, where xe (xℓ) is the consumption of early (late) producers, and ye (yℓ) is the production of early (late) producers. The function u(.) is continuous, increasing, concave, and u(0)=0. We assume that there are gains from trade between early and late producers: there exists x such that u(x)>x. We denote by x∗ the efficient allocation that solves u′(x∗)=1.Early and late producers meet pairwise at the start of the early production stage. The matching technology is such that nature selects a measure α of early and late producers and matches one with another pairwise. All other producers remain unmatched for the period. Therefore, the probability of a match for any producer is α. The match is maintained across both stages, but it dissolves at the end of the later stage.Feasibility and efficiency require that production equals consumption, i.e. xe=yℓ and xℓ=ye. Therefore, we can conveniently drop indices and use x≡xℓ=ye and y≡xe=yℓ. Hence, an allocation is (x,y) where x denotes the production of early producers (consumption of late producers), and y denotes the production of late producers (consumption of early producers). We concentrate on symmetric and stationary allocations. Fig. 1 sketches the timeline of our economy.We restrict trading mechanisms to be in the class of coordination games. For each match between an early and late producer, each producer announces a pair (x̃,ỹ)∈R+2. If both announcements coincide, the early producer produces x=x̃ in the early stage and the late producer produces y=ỹ in the late stage. In this case we say that the allocation (x,y) can be implemented.Download: Download high-res image (356KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Timeline.We assume late producers have only a limited ability to commit.24 Absent commitment and any record-keeping technology, it is routine to show that late producers will never produce for early producers, and the only implementable allocation is autarky (x,y)=(0,0).A ledger technologyTo discipline late producers it is necessary to record their history of trades in a ledger. Only then can late producers face the threat of losing future consumption if they do not produce today (see Kocherlakota (1998)). The ledger records 0̸ if producers have no match. Histories of producers can be conveniently summarized using two labels: good (G) or bad (B), as late producers can be in good or bad standing. Producers will be assigned label B whenever their actions differ from their announcements some time in the past, irrespective of how long ago this has happened. Otherwise, they will be assigned label G. Notice that label B is an absorbing label, and an agent carrying label B will never consume or produce.25Then, an allocation (x,y) is implementable if it is incentive feasible (IF): it satisfies participation constraints and late producers produce for the early producer. The two participation constraints are u(x)≥y and y≥x, while late producers will have the incentive to produce for early producers if their “repayment constraint” holds, −y+βαu(x)−y1−β≥0.If late producers do not produce, they are excluded from the economy, so the right-hand side of the constraint is zero. If they produce, they incur the production cost −y and are assigned a good label so they can trade in the future. The expected value of having a good label is equal to the discounted lifetime gains from trade times the probability of trading α. Setting y=x, the set of IF allocations is characterized by all x that satisfy βαu(x)≥(1−β(1−α))x.The efficient allocation x∗ is implementable if β and α are large enough.
article.3. Trustless ledgers: So far, our analysis has been routine because we have taken the functioning of the ledger as a given. The objective of our paper is to endogenize the ledger updating process, without the help of a trusted central authority, and explain the incentive problem arising from the mechanism used to update the ledger.To endogenize the updating process of the ledger, we assume the ledger is managed by a measure V of “validators”. Validators are rational agents who need incentives to verify transactions and update the ledger honestly. The resulting history should be trustworthy to all producers.We consider two types of validators: In period 0, a measure V of validators can be selected from the set of late producers (internal validation) or from a set of agents who are indifferent as to which good they consume (external validation).26 Each period, all V validators work on validating each of the α matches.27 We assume a validator uses the same strategy across all matches, as described below. Therefore, a validator either works on validating all matches or none. Exiting validators are replaced by new validators in a way we specify below. For symmetry we assume that external validators are also subject to a survival shock.There is a private cost to opening an account on the ledger (we show below that this is optimal):28 Late producers pay γ to open an account,29 while the cost to open an account eligible for validation is γv, where v=IV when there is internal validation and v=EV when there is external validation. Once an account is open, validators verify only the standing of that account, that is, whether the holder of an account defaulted in the past on the liabilities generated with that account.30 If late producers default on an account, that account is assigned label B and trade is no longer possible with it. Since the full identity of the holder of the account is not known, he or she can open another account. Opening a new account does not give information as to the reasons why that account is opened because there are always new agents who seek to use the ledger.Each period, the ledger collects Tℓ from new late producers who are opening accounts and Tv from new validators who are opening accounts. These collections are redistributed in a lump sum to, respectively, (continuing) late producers and (continuing) validators.31 Since there is no reason for a producer to open more than one account at a time, and for brevity, we will sometimes refer to the “standing of producer i” when referring to the “standing of the account held by producer i”.To simplify the analysis, we assume early producers can read the account label of late producers in the ledger, but they cannot write the outcome of the match on the ledger. Therefore, the validation process takes place in the late stage only.32The validation process consists of validators (1) verifying that late producers have produced according to plan, i.e. ỹ=y,33 and (2) sending a message to the ledger — if production took place according to plan and the late producer’s account has label G, validators will communicate G, but B otherwise. Consensus is reached on the new label, which is recorded on the ledger whenever at least a fraction τ∈[0,1] of the V validators communicate the same label, i.e. cast the same vote. We emphasize that τ is a choice variable when designing the consensus protocol.34Validators incur verification and communication costs. Validators incur a utility cost cv≥0 to verify a late producer’s action and an idiosyncratic additional utility cost cs,i to send a message to the ledger (or to enter their information on the ledger). Since it is costly to send messages, we assume validators send a message only when they want to communicate that the late producer’s label is G.35In order to model the possibility of computer glitches and operational failures, we assume that the private cost of communicating a label cs,i takes the form (1)cs,i=cs+μi,where cs is a common component to all validators uniformly distributed over c̲s,c̄s, while μi is the idiosyncratic element for validator i that is uniformly distributed over the interval ɛɛ[−ɛ,ɛ], where ɛɛ is a small positive number. For any two distinct validators i≠j, μi is independent of μj. Validators learn their costs ahead of the verification game and so ahead of verifying the label.To induce agents to become validators, they must make a positive expected profit from the validation process. Sending a message is verifiable, so validators who correctly sent message G are entitled to z units of the late good whenever enough validators agree that the label is G. Validators receive nothing if they do not cast a vote. Validators value these transfers in an additive and linear way.36 When a fraction w≥τ of validators send label G at the end of stage 2, the late producer has to produce wVz to compensate these validators. These validators then update the ledger.37Notice that validators play a game that is similar to a public good contribution game: they get a reward only if a sufficient number of validators confirm a trade. Given the structure of the validation costs, we solve this game using tools from the literature on global games. As Morris and Shin (2003) show, the key to the analysis is the characterization of the strategic uncertainty faced by players. Even if the idiosyncratic component is small relative to the other payoff parameters in the frame, the relative ranking of the costs injects strategic uncertainty in the coordination game. Although remote, the possibility of computer glitches will imply that validation should not rely on unanimous agreement when there are many validators. In particular, the equilibrium in the limiting case as ɛɛ→0 gives rise to some degree of coordination failure and associated inefficiencies, in contrast to the (common knowledge) case when ɛɛ=0, which is typically associated with multiple equilibria. Moving forward, we focus our attention to the limiting case of ɛɛ→0.We assume late producers can bribe validators to send a false message. A late producer whose account has the label G when starting the period may get away with not repaying the early producer while keeping the label by “bribing”, i.e. making a side payment to τV validators in the late stage (after consuming in the first stage).38 Validators who accept a bribe are caught with probability π, in which case their current account loses the privilege to trade and validate.39 However, those validators can open a new account, allowing them to trade and/or validate in the future.Finally, we assume late producers, including validators, have the option to switch to another ledger. We assume the benefit from trading on the other ledger is a fraction η≤β of their current expected payoff. This is similar to assuming that it is costly or it takes time to switch to another ledger, and that the role of the agent on the new ledger is the same as the one on the current ledger.40 Conveniently, setting η=0 takes us back to the baseline framework where agents cannot switch ledgers. We assume that information is not portable across ledgers. As a result, agents who are switching ledgers cannot distinguish themselves from new agents. Allowing agents to share their histories would complicate the analysis because the ledger pricing policy could be history-dependent, possibly leading to a different equilibrium, with history as a coordination device across ledgers. In Online Appendix 6 we consider an extension where the outside option is not related to the current expected payoff, and agents can choose between being validators or just late producers. These changes do not affect the qualitative results.We proceed with analyzing the model as follows. First, we define the expected utility of early and late producers as well as of the validators, given some allocations in Section 3.1. Then we characterize the set of stationary and symmetric allocations that satisfy the participation and incentive constraints of all agents, i.e. the incentive feasible allocations. These allocations must be such that late producers have the incentive to produce for early producers, which imposes a “repayment constraint”. Further, late producers must not have an interest in bribing validators to record a false entry in the ledger, which imposes a “no-bribing constraint”. Additionally, these allocations should make sure enough validators have the incentives to verify and validate the outcome in each match despite the private information regarding the communication cost. As is standard, in Section 3.2 we characterize incentive feasible allocations when the private information vanishes. This allows us to consider a fictitious planner’s choice of the best allocation among the set of incentive feasible allocations in Section 3.3. The planner’s objective function is to maximize the gains from trade between producers net of the validation costs. Therefore, this planner can be seen as a coalition of agents maximizing their expected utility under the veil of ignorance regarding their (future) type. Our main result is the characterization of the best incentive feasible allocation, i.e. the constrained optimal allocation. Since this allocation implies the number of validators, the validation threshold, the payment to validators, the production/consumption, and the fees, it describes the best design for the ledger. In Section 4, we show that an economy with competing ledgers designed by early producers implements the same allocation (or design) as the one chosen by the planner.3.1. Payoffs and incentive feasible allocationsGiven a threshold τ and a measure of validators V assigned to validate each match, a stationary allocation is a list of account fees and production/consumption (γ,γv,x,y,z). An allocation is incentive feasible if it is feasible, it satisfies the incentive constraints of early and late consumers (given τ), the labels of late producers’ accounts are correctly communicated to the ledger, and validators have no incentive to tamper with the record of labels.Given a stationary incentive feasible allocation (γ,γv,x,y,z), Ui is the expected discounted lifetime utility of late producer i with label G, satisfying Ui=Tℓ+Eiαu(x)−y+Iwi≥τ−wiVz+βUi+(1−α)βUi,where Ei is the expectation operator of late producer i over wi, the share of working validators. In this case, the late producer pays wiVz to the working validators and can trade in the future. With probability 1−α, the late producer does not trade but retains their label into next period. Otherwise they do not get the reward z.Similarly, the expected discounted lifetime utility of an external validator i with private communication cost cs,i is UEV(cs,i) and satisfies, (4)UEV(cs,i)=TEV+αmax0;Ei−cv+Iw≥τz−cs,i∣cs,i+βEUEV,where TEV is the transfer to external validators.Participation constraints. An allocation (γ,γv,x,y,z) satisfies the participation constraints of validators and early and late producers whenever, (5)−γ+U≥ηU,(6)−γv+Uv≥ηUv,(7)y−x≥0,(8)−cv+Ew≥τ∣cs,iz−cs,i≥0. Constraints (5), (6) require that late producers and validators are better off paying the account fee to use that ledger rather than using the competing ledger offering them ηU. Constraint (7) is the (simplified) participation constraint of the early producers. Finally, since late producers can shirk from validation, (8) requires that validators expect to make a positive expected profit from the validation process. The expectation operator in (8) applies to the share of working validators w.Repayment constraints. Using a ledger, in equilibrium late producers who do not produce the announced amount y are detected by validators and their account is thus assigned a label B. As a consequence, this account is permanently blocked from all economic activities. Similarly, an internal validator whose account is in default loses their future right to validate and their account is also blocked. Agents whose account has been blocked can open a new late producer’s account at cost γ or a validator’s account at cost γv, or obtain the payoff ηU by using the competing ledger (or opening a different account on the same ledger). Therefore, given the share of working validators is w≥τ, the repayment constraint of late producers and internal validators is, respectively, (9)−y+wVz+βU≥βmax{−γ+U;−γv+EUIV;ηU},(10)−y+wVz+βEUIV≥βmax{−γ+U;−γv+EUIV;ηU}.No bribe. If a validator accepts a bribe, we assume they are caught with probability π∈[0,1]. In this case they lose their right to validate future transactions and to consume as a late producer using their old account. Yet again, they can open a new account. A validator prefers recording the truth to a false record when the late producer offers z̄ iff βEUs≥z̄︸bribe+(1−π)βEUs+πβmax{Is=IV−γ+U;−γs+Us;ηUs},where s=IV,EV to denote internal of external validation, respectively. When a share w of validators are working on a match, the late producer in this match is willing to pay at most a total of y+wVz to get away with production. Given that the ledger requires the agreement of at least τV validators to validate a (fake) transaction, the cheating late producer will pay z̄=(y+wVz)/(τV) to τV validators. Therefore, a validator rejects the bribe whenever42 (11)πβEUs≥1τVy+wVz+πβmax{Is=IV−γ+U;−γs+Us;ηUs}.Finally, we need to identify a condition such that, given a threshold τ, the allocation allows for a truthful record of the ledger.Validation threshold. Suppose there is a positive measure of validators in charge of verifying each match. Then the decision of an arbitrary validator to work or to shirk depends on the subjective probability this validator assigns to other validators working or shirking. Therefore, there are many possible equilibria depending on the original beliefs of validators. In other words, the uncertainty about the cost to other validators of communicating a label to the ledger may reverberate throughout the system and may jeopardize the validation process.We assume the continuation payoff of validators is independent of the current validation results.43 Also if validators do not work, they do not send any messages, thus eliminating a possible free-rider problem. We relax these assumptions in Online Appendix 3, where we examine an extension that also specifies the voting game that validators play, and we state conditions under which validators do not send a message without working.Now suppose the validation process requires unanimity. As soon as validators expect a positive measure to abstain from validating, they will also abstain even though they may have received a signal that the communication cost is small. We show that, as a consequence, validation occurs correctly only when the validation rule is based on a supermajority, unless payments to validators are arbitrarily large. The higher the supermajority threshold, the more rents should accrue to validators in order to guarantee the integrity of the ledger. We show the following result in Appendix A.Proposition 1Given τ, in the limit as ɛɛ→0, there is a unique dominance-solvable equilibrium where validators work if and only if the allocation z satisfies (12)z≥cs+cv1−τ.The proof follows two steps. In the first step, we characterize equilibria when, for some common threshold cs∗, validators employ switching strategies whereby they work if their cost cs,i is below the threshold cs∗ and they shirk otherwise. A validator receiving a cost at threshold level cs∗ is indifferent to working and shirking.It is well known from the global game literature (e.g. Morris and Shin (2003)) that, for the marginal player whose cost is exactly equal to the threshold value cs∗, the density over the share of working agents is uniform over [0,1]. Hence, the validator assigns a probability q to the event that a fraction q of the V validators will work. Since this validator is indifferent between working or not, and her subjective beliefs of the share of working validators is uniform, i.e. g(τ∣cs∗)=1, cs∗ solves −cv+(1−τ)z−cs∗+τ−cs∗=0.When the noise vanishes, all individual costs necessarily converge to the common value cs. Therefore, when cs≤cs∗, all validators will work to validate a trade and the ledger will record labels correctly, while when cs>cs∗, none of them will. The allocation z logically affects the threshold value cs∗: by increasing the validators’ rents z, the validation protocol can ensure that validation happens for higher levels of the communication cost.Hence, the first step of the proof establishes that the validation game has a unique equilibrium in switching strategies. The second step of the proof establishes that this unique equilibrium in switching strategies is also the only strategy profile of the players that survives the iterated deletion of strictly dominated strategies. In other words, the game is dominance solvable.44As a corollary, notice that the payment z to validators is positively linked to the supermajority level τ when there are validation costs. Therefore, the ledger can retain integrity only when unanimity is required if payments to validators are arbitrarily large. Finally, given τ, the probability that the trade will go through when cs is uniformly distributed is the probability that cs≤(1−τ)z−cv≡cs∗.If the ledger is required to allow all legitimate trades involving a producer with label G to always go through, then z should be set to (13)z≥Z(τ)≡11−τc̄s+cv≡C1−τ,where c̄s is the maximum possible communication cost. In this case, and given τ, validators will always work. Below, we assume this is the case. In Online Appendix 4, we relax this assumption and analyze the optimal validation protocol for any thresholds cs∗, letting the designer choose what cs∗ should be. Also, we derive sufficient conditions under which cs∗=c̄s is optimal.We can now define incentive feasible allocations.Definition 2Given τ and V, an incentive feasible allocation is a list (γ,γv,x,y,z) that satisfies (5)–(11), (13).In what follows we solve for the optimal design of the validation protocol and how it affects incentive feasible allocations. We simplify matters further by assuming that the distribution for the communication cost cs converges to the degenerate distribution that gives all the mass to just one point c̄s.3.2. Limiting distribution of communication costsFor the remainder of the analysis we consider the global game-limiting case where ɛɛ→0, and (13) holds. In this limit, all validators face the same communication cost equal to c̄s. So unless they are bribed, all validators always verify that production took place according to plans, and they always cast the right vote to the ledger. Therefore, the share of working validators w is equal to one and we can write (5)–(12) with wi=1. We can further simplify the set of IF allocations by setting the participation constraint of early producers (7) at equality.In this section, we consider the incentives of a late producer to make side payments to validators so that they record a false trade, and we analyze the incentives of validators to accept that bribe. Since the payment to validators should be minimized, (13) binds so validators earn Z(τ) and the participation constraint of validators (8) is always satisfied. Also, the incentive constraints of late producers and validators are relaxed when the cost of opening an account is set as high as possible, that is γ=(1−η)U and γv=(1−η)Uv. Then denote the expected rent of validators as R(τ), (14)R(τ)=τZ(τ).Define the default factor as (recall that the effective discount factor is β=σβ̃) δ≡πσβ̃(1−η)α1−σβ̃−(1−η)(1−σ)/σ,and replace the expressions for the transfers, to find the set of IF allocations characterized by45 (15)δπu(x)−x−VZ(τ)≥x+VZ(τ),(16)δu(x)−x−VZ(τ)+R(τ)≥1τVx+VZ(τ). The higher δ is, the weaker the incentives of validators to accept a bribe are, because they would lose a lot of trading opportunities (as captured by a large α), they would very likely be caught cheating (as captured by a high π) or they care a lot about future income (as captured by a high β̃), or because the outside option is really poor (η is low). Also, the default factor can be increasing or decreasing in the survival rate σ. There are two counteracting effects from a higher survival rate: on one hand, agents effectively become more patient, which improves incentives; but on the other hand, a higher survival rate decreases the transfer that surviving agents get from the ledger, which weakens discipline. Which effect dominates depends on the strength of the outside option: if η is low enough, the default factor falls with a higher σ.According to constraint (15), late producers are better off retaining their good label by repaying x to early producers and VZ(τ) to validators than getting a bad label and losing the expected lifetime discounted payoff from trading net of the payment to validators. Constraint (16) states the condition for validators to reject a bribe: the payoff from accepting the maximum bribe a late producer offers, (x+VZ(τ))/τV, must be lower than the expected loss of accepting such a bribe, given they are caught with probability π. In addition to losing the expected lifetime discounted payoff from trading net of compensating validators, validators would also lose the expected rents they earn R(τ). With external validation, (16) simplifies to (17)δR(τ)≥1τVx+VZ(τ),Inequality (17) requires that losing future validation rents with probability π should be a larger cost to external validators than the benefit of accepting the largest bribe a late producer would offer.46We focus next on the number of validators V. The more validators there are, the higher the overall payment for validation — this is the term VZ(τ) on the left-hand side of (15). However, given τ, more validators also means a lower bribe for each validator, thus relaxing their incentive constraint as shown on the right-hand side of (16) or (17). The optimal number of validators will trade off both effects. When the measure of validators is large, the repayment constraint of late producers becomes the binding one when π→1, so that validators never accept a bribe. This is intuitive: when V is large, no validator gets a very large bribe; but when the mechanism almost surely observes when they accept a bribe, they almost certainly lose the expected lifetime payoff from trading. Then, only the incentives of the late producer matter. However, note that if (15) is binding while (16) is slack, V can be reduced up to the point where (16) binds. We summarize the discussion above in the following result.Lemma 3If π and V are large enough, validators will never accept a bribe.3.3. Optimal designThe objective of a planner is to maximize the gains from trade net of the validation costs. A planner chooses the costs to open accounts γ and γv, the trading size x, the type of validation (internal or external), the number of validators, and the threshold τ to solve (18)αmaxγ,x,V≥0,τ∈[0,1]u(x)−x−VC,subject to (15), (16) for internal validation or (17) for external validation. These constraints have been simplified by using γ=(1−η)U and γv=(1−η)Uv. But this is without loss of generality: the cost to open the account is the redistributed lump sum to all agents in the same period it is levied on late producers. Since this is a mere redistribution, it does not affect the objective of the planner. Also the higher the lump sum transferred to agents, the more relaxed their incentive constraints. Therefore, as we guessed above, the cost to open an account can be set as high as possible, that is as high as the lifetime benefit of trading on the ledger net of the available outside option. Conveniently, the size of the payment to validators Z(τ) only matters for incentives but has no impact on the objective function because it is a transfer between producers and validators. Also note that whether validation is internal or external does not affect the social planner’s objective function (18). However, comparing (16), (17) shows that as long as trade is sufficiently beneficial, or u(x)>(x+VZ), then (16) is always satisfied whenever (17) is. In other words, the set of incentive feasible allocations is larger with internal validation, and internal validation weakly dominates external validation. But not only this. As we show below, whenever trade can be supported, centralization is optimal with external validation. Next, we start by considering the optimal design with external validation.Optimal design with external validation.With external validation, the only relevant constraints are (15), (17), which we can write, respectively, as (19)δu(x)≥(δ+π)x+VC1−τ,(20)(1−τ)x≤δτ2−1VC. It is easy to show that the validators’ incentive constraint (20) always binds,47 so we can use it to replace for the total cost of validation, VC, in the late producer’s repayment constraint (19) and the objective function. The problem of the planner then becomes αmaxx,τ∈[τ̄,1]u(x)−δτ2−τδτ2−1x,subject to δu(x)≥(δ+π)δτ2δτ2−1x.Inspecting (20), notice that a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of external validation is that intertemporal incentives are strong enough, in the sense that δ≥1. Otherwise, the only incentive feasible allocation is autarky with V=0. With δ>1, it is straightforward to verify that the constraint is relaxed when τ is highest. Also, the objective function is maximized when τ=1. Therefore, with external validation, the solution is τ=1 and V→0, and the optimal trading size x̃(δ)≤x∗, where x̃(δ)=x∗ if δ≥δ∗>1, so that the constraint is not binding or it is defined as the solution to u(x̃)=x̃(δ+π)/(δ−1) otherwise, with x̃(δ)→0 as δ decreases to 1. The payment to the validator is VZ→x̃(δ)/(δ−1).Optimal design with internal validation.Next, consider the optimal design with internal validation. As we show in the Appendix, the social planner’s objective function (18) is decreasing in V, and so we obtain the following.Lemma 4With internal validation, the incentive constraint of validators (16) always binds, while the incentive constraint of late producers (15) never binds.Replacing the expression for the validation rent (14) in (16), and rearranging, we obtain: (21)δu(x)−x≥xτV+C1−τ1−δ(1−V)τ+δC.Since the objective function (18) is independent of τ, the planner chooses τ to minimize the right-hand side of (21). Two forces are at play. On one hand, increasing τ reduces the maximum bribe size per validator. On the other hand, increasing τ increases the payment to validators Z(τ) to ensure that validators indeed verify and validate. When intertemporal incentives are strong, so that 1≤δ1−V, this second effect reduces the right-hand side of (21): validators have much to lose by accepting a bribe. In this case, as with external validation, it is optimal to set τ=1, even if Z(τ)→∞. Alternatively, suppose intertemporal incentives are weak, 1>δ1−V, then the optimal τˆ trades off the smaller bribe size with the higher payment to the validator, and it solves48 (22)1−τˆτˆ=1−δ1−VVCx+VC.The optimal threshold τˆ is decreasing in V but increasing in x. τˆ is also increasing in π, β, or α, as captured by δ. The intuition is apparent from (15): when π, β, or α increase, the net rent R(τ)−VZ(τ)>0 of validators becomes more important for the incentives of validators relative to the bribe size (x+VZ)/τV, either because they have a higher chance of losing it – when π increases – or because they have a higher lifetime discounted value – when β or α increases. So following an increase in δ, the planner gives more net rent to validators by increasing τ. So unlike in traditional models of limited commitment, higher trustworthiness as captured by a higher δ implies more rents to validators.Importantly, while external validation implements autarky only when intertemporal incentives are weak, internal validation can do much more. This is intuitive: with internal validation, the planner can use the value of trading in the future to discipline validators, thus allowing the planner to choose lower rents (and therefore a lower feasible bribe) that validators obtain from being able to manage the ledger.The optimal choice of x and V trades off several effects. First, increasing x toward x∗ brings additional gains from trade, but at the cost of increasing the bribe size per validator, which tightens their incentive constraint. Second, increasing V relaxes the incentive constraint of validators, but increases the cost of validation. We can now summarize these considerations in our main result.Proposition 5The constrained optimal solution (xˆ,Vˆ,τˆ) solves (21) at equality and (22)–(26) and is characterized by four regions:1a. [centralized system - efficient trade size] If δ≥δ∗>1, external and internal validations are characterized by Vˆ→0, τˆ→1 and xˆ→x∗. Validation requires arbitrarily large payments, i.e. Z(τˆ)→∞, while limτˆ→1V(τˆ)Z(τˆ)=x∗δ−1.Welfare is higher with internal validation.1b. [centralized system - inefficient trade size] If 1≤δ≤δ∗, external validation is characterized by Vˆ→0, τˆ→1 and xˆ→x̃(δ)≤x∗. Internal validation is characterized by Vˆ>0, τˆ<1 and xˆ<x∗. Welfare is higher with internal validation.2. [partially distributed system] If δ̄<δ≤1, only internal validation can decentralize trade. The constrained optimal number of validators is Vˆ>0, and only a supermajority τˆ<1 is optimal. Each validator receives a finite payment Z(τˆ)<∞. The constrained optimal allocation is xˆ<x∗.3. [fully distributed system] If δ0<δ≤δ̄, only internal validation can decentralize trade. All late producers are validators Vˆ=1, and τˆ=1+Cx+C−1. The constrained optimal allocation is xˆ<x∗.4. [no trade] If δ≤δ0, there is no validation protocol that can decentralize trade.Proposition 5 states that the optimal design of the ledger requires centralized validation by a single validator only when validators are sufficiently trustworthy. However, moving toward centrality necessarily imposes a move toward unanimity. In the limit, an arbitrarily large payment must be made to the single authority managing the ledger. In reality, feasible payments may be limited and, in such a case, a single validator will never be optimal. Still, the single validator case offers a useful benchmark that illustrates the forces driving the solution toward centrality. When intertemporal incentives become weak, the single authority can be more easily convinced to do wrong and the optimal validation protocol moves away from centrality. The centrifugal forces also manifest themselves in a reduction in trade size and a departure from unanimity or a high supermajority rule. Both margins reduce the feasible bribe to validators and therefore their incentives to do wrong.49Underlying the results in Proposition 5 are the following comparative statistics (details of the calculations are in the Appendix): x and τ are (weakly) increasing with δ (i.e. β, π, and α) but decreasing with validation costs C. V is (weakly) decreasing with δ but increasing with validation costs C.As the level of patience or frequency of trading among validators increases (represented by a higher δ), the optimal number of validators (V) decreases while the optimal trade size (x) increases. This is intuitive because when validators are more patient or trade more frequently, they have more to lose from any misdeeds. Therefore, as δ increases the planner can increase the size of each trade and reduce the number of validators needed. This choice of V and x indirectly affects τ and reinforces the planner’s decision to increase the net rent for validators by choosing a larger threshold, as we already explained above. All in all, these effects work together to increase the threshold τ with δ.50Centrifugal forces also include the cost of validation C. This may be surprising, as increasing the number of validators also increases duplication costs to the detriment of welfare. However, higher validation costs reduce the overall lifetime discounted surplus of validators, who as a result are more easily convinced to do wrong. To control this effect, the planner reduces the feasible bribe (given x) that a later producer can pay by increasing the number of validators.Reaching consensus with a higher number of validators however does not come for free: as validation cost C is higher, validators are more likely to believe that fewer other validators will work. Hence, maintaining the same level of consensus requires that each validator receives a larger rent, as Proposition 1 shows. Therefore, to maintain the legitimacy of the ledger while keeping costs in check, the threshold τ should fall. Fig. 2 illustrates Proposition 5 setting η=0 so that the value of the outside option is zero. Given some x≤x∗ and the default factor δ, Fig. 2 shows the set of (V,τ) for which the IC of validators (21) is satisfied. When δ is relatively high, x=x∗ and (V,τ)=(0,1) satisfy (21) and this is the best design for the system. In this case, the solution is given by the black dot in the lower right corner of Fig. 2(a). In contrast, Fig. 2(b) shows incentive feasible allocations when δ is relatively smaller: then the allocation x=x∗ and (V,τ)=(0,1) is no longer incentive feasible. The figure shows that implementing x=x∗ is feasible for some (V,τ), but only for relatively large V. This is costly and the planner does better by choosing a lower x which decreases the bribe size and allows it to select fewer validators (lower the number of validators V), as shown by the red area in Fig. 2(b). Reducing x slightly below x∗, the planner only makes a second order loss, but realizes a first order gain as V decreases, thus reducing the overall validation costs. Therefore, as δ decreases, x declines and (V,τ) moves up along the black curve, as shown in Fig. 2(c). As δ falls below the threshold δ̄ toward δ0, V=1 and τ moves westward on the black line until it reaches 1/2 and x=0. For levels of δ below δ0, there is no equilibrium with trade. Implementation with competing ledgers: In this section we show that competing ledgers can implement the planner’s allocation analyzed above.51One issue when considering competing ledgers is that, to be profitable, the ledgers must attract both early and late producers. This is a typical two-sided market problem, where the participation of one side (early producers) encourages the participation of the other side (late producers). The pricing can be complex, as one side could be subsidized at the cost of the other (see Rochet and Tirole, 2006). We sidestep this issue by assuming that coalitions of early producers are setting up ledgers. We also do not take a stand on how these coalitions are formed. Instead, we show that competing ledgers can implement the social planner’s optimal design for any number of ledgers. Suppose there are N coalitions consisting of a measure 1/N of early producers, competing for the membership of late producers. We assume that each coalition can set up only one ledger so that there are N possible ledgers indexed by n=1,…,N. We suppose it costs K to set up any account on any of the ledgers.52To compensate for the cost of setting up an account on a ledger, the coalition charges a fee to late producers. Late producers apply to a ledger before knowing if they will be selected as validators. If they are selected as validators, they pay γv to the ledger; if they are not, they pay γ. As before, we use V to denote the fraction of late producers selected to be validators (for any measure of producers) on a particular ledger. Notice that competing on account fees is not the only thing that ledgers do because, depending on their design, ledgers offer different lifetime utility to their users. However, below we show that there is a clear relationship between account fees and the utility of users.Ledgers are competing as follows. Suppose the design of ledger n offers expected lifetime utility U to a late producer and Uv to a validator and charges the respective fees γ and γv. Then, after a late producer learns his role on ledger n but before he pays the fee to ledger n, he receives an offer from ledger i≠n that gives him ηiU as a late producer and ηiUv as a validator. We require that the offer ηi be feasible given the design of ledger i: if ledger i offers a fee γi and an expected utility Ui, then ηiU≤Ui−γi. Let η̄n=maxi≠nηi be the highest competing offer faced by users of ledger n.Then the objective of ledger n is to choose γ, γv, and V to maximize the lifetime discounted value of these transfers,53 1+β1−β(1−σ)(1−V)γ+Vγv,subject to the account setup costs (1−V)γ+Vγv≥K and where the participation constraints of producers and validators are satisfied, −γ+U≥η̄nU,−γv+Uv≥η̄nUv, where, as before, U and Uv are the expected lifetime utility of a late producer and a validator. Notice that if ledger n chooses V validators and a design that guarantees utility U to late producers and Uv to validators, with costs γ and γv, then the net expected utility of a late producer applying to ledger n is Un≡(1−V)−γ+U+V−γv+Uv. Inspecting the ledger’s problem, it should be clear that the participation constraints will hold with equality, so the objective function of ledger n is proportional to (1−V)U+VUv. Therefore, the objective of the ledger is proportional to (1−V)(u(x)−x−VZ)+V(u(x)−x−VZ)+(Z−C)=u(x)−x−VC, where we have used the same steps as in the main text to simplify the expected utilities. Therefore, the objective function of any ledger is the same as the planner’s. This implies that all ledgers always adopt the planner’s optimal design (x,V,Z) that maximizes the net trading surplus given η̄n.Next we specify how ledgers compete over the promised expected utility to late producers, Un for all n. The game is as follows: Ledgers n=1,…,N simultaneously announce an expected utility Ūn and a factor {ηi}i≠n. Following the announcement, late producers select the ledger that offers the highest expected utility. If late producers are indifferent between n ledgers, we assume that each of the n ledgers serves a fraction 1/n of them. A symmetric Nash equilibrium of this game is defined as a pair Ū,η̄ such that all ledgers announce Ū,η̄ given they expect all other ledgers to announce Ū,η̄. The promise Ū has to be feasible, that is, there are fees γ and γv such that (1−V)γ+Vγv≥K as well as −γ+U≥η̄U and −γv+Uv≥η̄Uv, where U and Uv are the expected utility of late producers and validators given η̄.In a symmetric Nash equilibrium, Ūn=Ū for all n, all ledgers choose the optimal design (x,V,Z) and therefore offer the same expected utility to late producers (U) and to validators (Uv). Given this, we show that a Nash equilibrium is characterized by Ū=(1−V)U+VUv−AK. The fees γ and γv are set such that γ=(1−η̄)U, γv=(1−η̄)Uv, and η̄ solves (1−η̄)(1−V)U+VUv=K (where V,U, and Uv are functions of η̄).To show this result, consider the strategy of ledger 1, given all other ledgers are posting Ū,η̄. If U1<Ū, ledger 1 does not get any late producers, which cannot be optimal. Can U1>Ū? If it was, there would be fees (γ1,γv,1) such that (1−V)−γ1+U+V−γv,1+Uv>(1−V)U+VUv−K, which is not feasible, as this implies K>(1−V)γ1+Vγv,1. Therefore, the only feasible/optimal choice is U1=Ū. Next, consider the choice of η̄. If ledger 1 sets η<η̄, then it is not competing because all other ledgers offer the higher share η̄. Now consider ledger 1 offering η>η̄. Given that all other ledgers offer η̄, ledger 1’s optimal design is (U,γ) and (Uv,γv) such that −γ+U=η̄U,−γv+Uv=η̄Uv.Therefore, η>η̄ is not consistent with the design of ledger 1.54
article.5. Conclusion: In this paper, we have presented an economic analysis of decentralized ledger technology in an economy where money is essential. To our knowledge, our analysis is the first economic analysis of DLT in such a context. It links a ledger validation game to monetary exchange, establishes the uniqueness of the equilibrium via a global game approach, and characterizes the optimal supermajority voting rule, number of validators, and size of transactions.We believe our analysis is timely, as DLT is rapidly becoming an industry standard for digital currencies and in other applications. In particular, our results can shed light on the burgeoning literature on central bank digital currency (CBDC) and stablecoins insofar as it gives conditions under which a central authority should manage the ledger of transactions.55 The discussion of technology and the economics of central bank digital money has thus far centered on the balance sheet effects and related systemic implications.56 Here, we focus not on balance sheets and the issue of how the value of a currency can be guaranteed (central backing is of the essence for a CBDC irrespective of our analysis), but on the governance of a societal record-keeping device used as a substitute for money.Of course, we have made simplifying assumptions in order to better grasp the basic economics of money as memory. Future work should relax some of these. For instance – to simplify the analysis – we have assumed that validators all agree to accept bribes in unison. It would be interesting to also study the cooperative games between validators in more detail. We have also taken as given that agents use a private ledger, as they want to preserve their anonymity in trades. Tirole (2020) and Chiu and Koeppl (2020) make progress on this front. In order to better compare the different types of ledger, future work should also include the benefit from preserving anonymity. Also our mechanism design approach implies that we have ignored every industrial organization aspect of DLT, which might be significant if this technology were to be widely adopted in the future.
